sources.exact_document_title: DHA Telehealth Clinical Guidelines for Virtual Management of Depression
sources.population: Adults presenting with suspected or established depressive symptoms; emergency, severe, psychotic, bipolar, or safeguarding presentations require escalation.
sources.clinical_setting: Telehealth services in DHA-licensed health facilities.
sources.applicability_note: Direct for Dubai telehealth depression documentation and risk screening; treatment, diagnosis, and local emergency pathways remain clinician-led.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 5–7 — depressive features and structured assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports low mood, loss of interest, sleep, appetite, energy, concentration, guilt or worthlessness, duration, function, previous episodes and treatment, medical and medication history, stressors, social context, comorbidity, mania or hypomania, substance use, family history, and support assessment.

exact_sections.heading: Section 7 — risk, mental-status examination, and selected investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician assessment of suicide, self-harm, harm or neglect, protective and support factors, mental status, cognition, insight, substance use, medical contributors, medication history, thyroid testing, and selected blood testing when indicated.

exact_sections.heading: Section 7 — red flags
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Lists suicidal ideation or attempt, risk of harm or neglect, moderate or severe depression, and psychotic or bipolar features as escalation concerns.

exact_sections.heading: Differential diagnosis — adjustment disorder with depressed mood
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Describes depressed mood in response to an identifiable psychosocial stressor and supports documenting single, multiple, recurrent, or continuing social and occupational stressors.

exact_sections.heading: Sections 8–9 — clinician-led management, monitoring, and referral
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented supportive discussion, questions, safety planning, treatment or medication decisions, monitoring, follow-up, and referral without autonomous treatment selection or doses.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Generalised anxiety disorder and panic disorder in adults: management — Recommendations
sources.population: Adults with known or suspected generalised anxiety disorder or panic disorder.
sources.clinical_setting: Primary, community, and specialist mental-health care.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for adult GAD identification, assessment, stepped care, risk, and clinician-led management; not exact for children, every anxiety subtype, or UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.2–1.2.11 — identification, assessment, education, and monitoring
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports anxiety and worry history, duration, distress, functional impairment, comorbid depression or anxiety, substance use, medical and treatment history, education, and active monitoring.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.17–1.2.33 and 1.2.44–1.2.48 — clinician-led treatment and shared decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented discussion of psychological or medication options, patient preference, treatment information, monitoring, and referral or relapse planning without autonomous treatment output.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.37–1.2.39 — high risk and specialist assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting severe functional impairment, self-harm or suicide risk, substance misuse, self-neglect, treatment history, home and community support, family impact, referral, and collaborative care planning.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.1–1.3.5 — diagnosis, presentation, and exclusion of acute physical problems
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured adult panic-disorder diagnosis, history, comorbidity review, panic presentations, and proportionate investigation to exclude acute physical problems before primary-care follow-up.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.6–1.3.25 — clinician-led treatment and shared decisions
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-documented discussion of psychological and medication options, patient preference, information, monitoring, self-help, referral, and follow-up without autonomous treatment output or doses.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.3.26–1.3.33 — reassessment, risk, and specialist care
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports reassessment of prior treatment and concordance, substance use, comorbidity, function, social support, stressors, avoidance, comprehensive risk, side effects, efficacy, and a shared care plan.
